items.text: Silk Road. I'd never had Uzbek food. I ordered lagman, samsa, and a kebab, because that's what the menu seemed to want me to do. The kebab was terrible. The lagman rearranged something. Spiced in a way I had no reference for, thick with vegetables, noodles with real life in them — I said at the time that I could have had that broth all day long, and I stand by it. I also said the interior looked like someone had opened Google Maps, dropped the street-view figure into Uzbekistan, and built what they saw: white tile, geometry everywhere. I ate in my car and drove home changed, in the small real way food occasionally changes you.

items.text: That could have stayed an anecdote. What made it a project was a decision I made within the week, half joke and fully serious: I would spend the winter eating every lagman I could reach in my corner of the region and produce a definitive ranking. I called it the Lagman Log, obviously.

segments.text: That could have stayed an anecdote. What made it a project was a decision I made within the week, half joke and fully serious: I would spend the winter eating every lagman I could reach in my corner of the region and produce a definitive ranking. I called it the Lagman Log, obviously.

items.text: The second bowl taught me the first lesson. Different restaurant, same nominal dish — and it was a different food. Heavy, gamey with lamb, barely spiced, served in a room that felt like a Russian wooden box, warm and rustic where Silk Road had been white and geometric. Also: everyone in that room was there in groups, eating that food the way it's meant to be eaten, together and loudly, and I was alone with a notebook. Still good. Not the same dish. And that gap — between two bowls both called lagman — turned out to be the actual subject. I hadn't discovered a soup. I'd discovered an axis. On one end, Uyghur cooking: spice-forward, halal, vegetable-rich, rooms that quote Islamic geometry, a crescent moon on the sign. On the other, the Soviet-Russian adaptation: meat-heavy, dill-inflected, comfort food for a different homesickness. And between and beside them, Bukharian Jewish kitchens keeping the same techniques kosher. One word, wearing at least three histories. Nobody handed me that; the two bowls did, by disagreeing — the knowledge lived in the gap between them and I only had to stand there and hold both.

On the other, the Soviet-Russian adaptation: meat-heavy, dill-inflected, comfort food for a different homesickness. Nobody handed me that; the two bowls did, by disagreeing — the knowledge lived in the gap between them and I only had to stand there and hold both.

items.text: So the ranking became a taxonomy, and here I have to admit what kind of person I am. A single 1-to-10 score was never going to survive contact with what I was actually tasting — not because I'm thorough but because a number is a preset box, and the boxes are exactly where my kind of noticing goes to die. So I built axes: cultural register on a spectrum rather than in boxes, spice complexity separate from spice heat, fat level, meat character, noodle integrity, how the vegetables were treated, and an overall harmony score for the way a bowl either coheres or doesn't. The axes weren't a decision so much as the only shape the attention could take. Then I built the instrument to hold it — a scoring interface with the full parametric system and an archive of entries. It was the first artifact I ever built in Claude. I want that on the record, plainly, because it's become a hinge in my own history: before the Log, I used these tools to think; the Log was the first time I used one to make — a small purpose-built machine for paying attention to soup. Everything I'm building now — and I'm building much stranger things now — descends from that bowl-rating widget the way cathedrals descend from someone stacking two stones. (The artifact itself is here https://anthonymichael.work/assets/, if you want to see a taxonomy nobody asked for, executed with total sincerity.)

items.text: Then the Log taught me its second lesson, on the night of what was supposed to be the inaugural official entry. I'd found a gorgeous Uyghur spot, arrived on a Sunday night, sat down ready to document — and they were out of lagman. Out. The instrument was ready and the phenomenon didn't show. And what I noticed, sitting there eating what they did have, is that the absence made me a better observer than the plan would have. I wrote afterward that I would probably have been too consumed with rating the lagman to notice anything else — the room, the hour, who else eats at that hour, what a restaurant open late is actually for. The expectation got decentered and the noticing widened. I ended up writing a line I still don't fully understand but haven't been able to improve on: we are being logged by lagman. The dish keeps its own record — of night workers, of the lonely and the hungry, of everyone a lit doorway at that hour is holding — and my log is a footnote to its log. That was the point where the project stopped being about ranking and I let it stop.

items.text: One more entry from the record, because it turned out to be a prophecy. In February, thinking about what the dish actually is, I wrote: I don't think I'll be eating this soup in July. It's July. I haven't. Not from discipline — I'd eat a bowl right now if you put one in front of me — but the Log knew something I was only just articulating: lagman is winter food, and the tracking had made its seasonality legible to me before I'd consciously registered it. That's the quiet payoff of keeping a record of anything. The data develops opinions. Eventually it tells you things about your own life that you were living but not seeing — that you eat this food alone, at night, in the cold months, in rooms built by people carrying whole geographies, and that this pattern is itself a fact about you worth witnessing.

items.text: The Log is still open, and this year it changes.

segments.text: The Log is still open, and this year it changes.

items.text: If you're in New York and want to eat this food, The Infatuation's Uyghur guide is a fine door in. If you're near Philadelphia — drive at night, keep your eyes open, and order the lagman.

items.text: The words are yours. The plumbing is mine.

What is it?

items.text: It's collage. It's cinema. It's phtoography. It's knowledge stored and encountered.
